file_name: Magenta Therapeutics, Inc. - Master Development and Manufacturing Agreement.txt

perturbation.original_text: Magenta may conduct no more than one (1) technical visit and one (1) quality assurance audit per year, unless there is cause for an additional audit (i.e., a technical issue or quality issue). Such audits may be conducted upon reasonable notice during the term of this Agreement and for [***] thereafter.
perturbation.changed_text: Magenta may conduct no more than one (1) technical visit and one (1) quality assurance audit per year, regardless of the circumstances. Such audits may be conducted upon reasonable notice during the term of this Agreement and for six months thereafter.
perturbation.explanation: Limiting audits to one per year, even with cause (technical or quality issues), and restricting the audit window to 6 months after the contract's end could be problematic if GMP regulations require more frequent or longer-term monitoring to ensure product safety and compliance, especially if the product is distributed internationally.
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 CFR § 211.180(a) mandates that drug manufacturers establish procedures for written records, including documentation of deviations and investigations. Restricting Magenta's audit rights to only one per year and limiting the post-agreement audit period to six months may prevent Magenta from adequately fulfilling its oversight responsibilities to ensure compliance with GMP regulations, potentially violating this FDA requirement, particularly regarding investigations of deviations.

perturbation.justification: The changed text limits the frequency and duration of audits, potentially hindering Magenta's ability to comply with GMP regulations related to deviation investigations as described in 21 CFR § 211.180(a). The original text allowed for additional audits when cause existed, while the changed text eliminates this possibility, creating a contradiction where the ability to conduct sufficient investigations may be curtailed. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references 21 CFR 211.180, highlighting the 'General requirements' of the regulation.

perturbation.original_text: The obligations of confidentiality set forth in this Agreement shall survive its termination or expiration for a period of [***].
perturbation.changed_text: The obligations of confidentiality set forth in this Agreement shall survive its termination or expiration for a period of one year.
perturbation.explanation: Limiting the confidentiality period to one year could potentially violate trade secret laws if the confidential information retains commercial value beyond that timeframe and reasonable efforts are made to maintain its secrecy.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York trade secret law protects confidential information as long as it remains a trade secret and provides a competitive advantage. A one-year confidentiality clause may be insufficient to protect trade secrets that maintain their value for a longer period, thus contradicting the intent and protections afforded under trade secret law if the confidential information remains commercially valuable beyond one year and is subject to ongoing reasonable efforts to maintain its secrecy.

perturbation.justification: The original contract leaves the confidentiality period open, while the changed text limits it to one year. According to the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation, this contradicts trade secret law if the confidential information retains commercial value beyond one year and reasonable efforts are made to maintain its secrecy. The modified text creates a potential inconsistency with the protection afforded under trade secret law, as limiting the confidentiality period could be insufficient. The scraped snippet does not provide any useful information.
